does: This paper introduces a post-training optimization stage for autoregressive speech enhancement language models that directly optimizes perceptual quality metrics rather than token-level cross-entropy. Using Group Sequence Policy Optimization (GSPO) with multi-metric perceptual rewards, the method jointly optimizes non-differentiable metrics including DNSMOS, WER, and UTMOS without requiring learned surrogate models or offline preference datasets. Applied to two base models (UniSE and GenSE), the approach achieves state-of-the-art performance on the DNS2020 benchmark and demonstrates through human evaluation that composite multi-metric rewards outperform single-metric optimization, effectively mitigating reward hacking behavior.
connects: This work builds on recent advances in discrete token-based speech enhancement using autoregressive language models, extending prior approaches that treated speech enhancement as sequence generation over discrete audio codes. It responds to the fundamental misalignment between training objectives (token-level cross-entropy) and evaluation metrics (perceptual quality scores) that has limited previous speech enhancement systems. The paper connects to broader trends in reinforcement learning from human feedback and reward optimization in language models, adapting policy optimization techniques like GSPO from language model alignment work to the speech domain while directly leveraging multi-metric rewards similar to multi-objective optimization approaches used in other audio and speech tasks.
enables: This work opens pathways for more effective post-training of speech enhancement and audio generation models by demonstrating that direct metric optimization through policy methods can surpass standard supervised training. The multi-metric optimization framework suggests future directions for balancing competing objectives in audio tasks without engineering single-metric solutions. The approach could extend to other speech processing tasks including speech separation, music enhancement, or voice conversion where perceptual evaluation metrics are available but non-differentiable. Additionally, the demonstrated effectiveness of composite rewards provides evidence for multi-objective optimization strategies in other domains, potentially influencing how language models and audio systems are fine-tuned beyond initial pre-training.
